rule_text: If Equation 1 is exactly v = T and variable v does not occur anywhere in T, then any model satisfying Equation 1 collapses to a singleton magma, so Equation 2 is automatically true.

If the variable sets of the two sides of Equation 1 are disjoint and Equation 1 contains binary terms, Equation 1 is strong evidence that the operation collapses to a constant behavior; many Equation 2 forms then become automatically true.
Do not apply singleton-collapse reasoning when Equation 1 shares variables across both sides, including self-referential shapes such as x = T(x, ...).
Treat Equation 2 as a true consequence of Equation 1 only when there is an exact renaming or exact substitution instance, not merely structural resemblance.
